title: Spotting an Attack on the Person

sentences.text: Dana proposed moving the team to a four-day week and shared a one-page summary of the trial data.
Of course Dana would say that — she's lazy and just wants more days off.
We should look at whether the trial actually improved output before deciding.

expected.rationale: Instead of engaging with Dana's data, the speaker attacks Dana's character ('lazy') and motive. That is a textbook Ad Hominem.

hints.text: Look for a Personal Attack — does someone go after the speaker instead of the idea?
Focus on the middle sentence, where Dana herself is described.
Calling Dana 'lazy' to dismiss her proposal is Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: Raj laid out a detailed plan to cut software costs by consolidating our tools.
Why would we listen to Raj about money — he drives a beat-up car and can't manage his own finances.
Let's check whether the consolidation actually saves what he claims.

expected.rationale: The speaker dismisses Raj's plan by mocking his personal finances rather than addressing the numbers. That is an Ad Hominem.

hints.text: Look for a Personal Attack — is someone judged instead of their argument?
The middle sentence is about Raj himself, not his plan.
Attacking Raj's car and finances to reject his plan is Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: Mia presented research suggesting the checkout flow should be shortened to two steps.
We can ignore Mia's opinion — she's fresh out of school and obviously doesn't know the real world.
The usability data she gathered is worth reviewing on its own merits.

expected.rationale: Mia's research is waved off by pointing at her age and inexperience instead of her findings. That is an Ad Hominem.

hints.text: Look for a Personal Attack — is the person targeted instead of the evidence?
The middle sentence is about Mia, not her data.
Dismissing Mia as 'fresh out of school' to reject her work is Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: We can dismiss the auditor's findings — she's clearly just chasing a promotion.

expected.rationale: It rejects the findings by guessing at her motive instead of checking them.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1.
"an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: His traffic study means nothing; the man can't even parallel park.

expected.rationale: It mocks the author rather than engaging the study.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1.
"an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: Why trust the chef's nutrition advice when he's overweight himself?

expected.rationale: It judges the advice by the speaker's appearance, not its substance.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1.
"an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: A friend of mine doubled her money on that coin, so it's a safe bet.
Half the office already uses this tool, so you should switch too.

expected.rationale: One lucky case is treated as proof the investment is safe.
Adoption by coworkers is used to pressure the choice.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Framing & Bias.
Focus on sentence 1, 2.
"a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence; "popularity treated as proof" → Bandwagon.

sentences.text: My cousin recovered fast after the surgery, so the procedure is clearly low-risk.
This track is topping every chart, so it must be a masterpiece.

expected.rationale: A single recovery stands in for real risk data.
Chart position is treated as proof of quality.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Framing & Bias.
Focus on sentence 1, 2.
"a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence; "popularity treated as proof" → Bandwagon.

sentences.text: Our top salesperson skips meetings and still wins, so meetings are pointless.
Millions can't be wrong about this show, so it's worth your time.

expected.rationale: One exception is generalized into a rule for everyone.
Popularity is offered as proof of merit.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Framing & Bias.
Focus on sentence 1, 2.
"a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence; "popularity treated as proof" → Bandwagon.

sentences.text: Either we adopt the new software company-wide today or we fall hopelessly behind.

expected.rationale: Two extremes are offered with no gradual rollout in between.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1.
"only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: You either support the highway expansion or you want the town to choke on traffic.

expected.rationale: It frames the choice as expansion or disaster, ignoring alternatives.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1.
"only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: We can either keep every legacy feature or rebuild the whole app from scratch.

expected.rationale: It excludes the option of incremental change.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1.
"only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: So you want flexible hours? I guess nobody should ever have to show up at all.

expected.rationale: It inflates a flexibility request into 'no one shows up.'

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1.
"a distorted version of the other side" → Straw Man.

sentences.text: You support trimming the budget, so clearly you want the whole department gone.

expected.rationale: It exaggerates a trim into total elimination.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1.
"a distorted version of the other side" → Straw Man.

sentences.text: She suggested a small pilot, so apparently she wants to experiment on everyone.

expected.rationale: It distorts a small pilot into reckless experimentation.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1.
"a distorted version of the other side" → Straw Man.

sentences.text: The intern's security warning isn't worth reading — he's barely out of his teens.
Either students memorize everything or they learn nothing at all.

expected.rationale: It dismisses the warning based on age, not its content.
It presents two extremes as the only learning outcomes.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack, Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1, 2.
"an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem; "only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: She opposes the rezoning only because she's a bitter landlord, so ignore her.
Either we pay for the premium tier or we accept a broken website.

expected.rationale: It explains away the objection through a supposed motive.
It ignores middle-ground options like a basic plan.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack, Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1, 2.
"an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem; "only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: Don't take investment tips from him; he still lives with his parents.
You're either fully vegan or you simply don't care about the planet.

expected.rationale: It attacks the person's living situation instead of the tips.
It collapses a spectrum into two rigid camps.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack, Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1, 2.
"an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem; "only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: We repainted the storefront and sales jumped, so the paint brought customers in.
The committee published its methodology alongside the results.

expected.rationale: Cause is read from timing alone.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: I switched to oat milk and my headaches stopped, so dairy caused them.
Analysts noted the sample skewed slightly younger than the population.

expected.rationale: It assumes the change must be the cause.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: The team adopted standups and shipped faster, so standups made them faster.
The vendor agreed to a trial period before any long-term contract.

expected.rationale: It infers cause purely from sequence.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: Approve the merger now, or we'll be bankrupt by spring.
Maintenance logs were cross-checked against the sensor data.

expected.rationale: A threat of bankruptcy replaces an argument.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: Skip the upgrade and hackers will drain your accounts overnight.
The forecast lists a margin of error for each estimate.

expected.rationale: It uses a frightening scenario instead of evidence.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: Vote against this and watch your neighborhood fall apart.
Reviewers flagged two assumptions that still need testing.

expected.rationale: It pressures with a dire image rather than reasons.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: She wore the lucky jersey and the team won, so the jersey won the game.
The professor's theory is laughable — he's been divorced twice, after all.
Don't question the plan, or you'll be the reason we all fail.
The pilot will be measured against a matched control group.

expected.rationale: It credits an event based only on order.
It drags in irrelevant personal facts to discredit the theory.
It uses fear of blame to silence questions.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors, Personal Attack, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc; "an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: Right after the ad aired, signups doubled, so the ad doubled them.
Their safety report is garbage because their CEO is so arrogant.
Cancel the program and a generation of kids will be left behind.
Costs were itemized so the board could compare options.

expected.rationale: Timing alone is treated as proof of cause.
It targets the CEO's personality, not the report's data.
A catastrophic prediction stands in for a case.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors, Personal Attack, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc; "an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: We added a mascot and donations rose, so the mascot drove the giving.
You can ignore her climate talk; she takes private jets sometimes.
Delay the recall and the headlines will destroy us all.
The survey was repeated in three regions to check consistency.

expected.rationale: It treats 'after' as 'because.'
It uses the speaker's behavior to dodge the argument.
It invokes disaster to force quick action.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors, Personal Attack, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc; "an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: You questioned one statistic, so you must reject all of science.
Ignore this warning and you may not have a job next month.
Staff documented the steps so others could reproduce them.

expected.rationale: It blows a single question up into rejecting science.
It threatens loss rather than giving reasons.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a distorted version of the other side" → Straw Man; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: He wants bike lanes, so he obviously thinks cars should be banned outright.
Reject the treaty and the region will erupt into chaos.
The team scheduled a follow-up audit for next quarter.

expected.rationale: It recasts bike lanes as an outright car ban.
It substitutes a frightening forecast for argument.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a distorted version of the other side" → Straw Man; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: You'd like more code review, so you think developers can't be trusted at all.
Refuse the policy and your savings could vanish in a crash.
Each figure in the report cites its original source.

expected.rationale: It twists a process request into total distrust.
It leans on fear of ruin instead of evidence.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a distorted version of the other side" → Straw Man; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: I tried the cold plunge once and felt great, so it must cure burnout.
Crime dropped once the cameras went up, so the cameras stopped the crime.
The proposal sets clear criteria for calling the project a success.

expected.rationale: A single experience is offered as proof of a broad effect.
It infers cause from the timing of the drop.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence; "cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: A startup near us succeeded without funding, so any company can.
He changed his diet and aced the exam, so the diet boosted his grades.
Engineers stress-tested the design before sign-off.

expected.rationale: One outlier is used to claim a general possibility.
It links two events by sequence, not evidence.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence; "cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: My grandmother ate butter daily and lived to ninety, so the guidelines are wrong.
Traffic eased after the new signal, so the signal fixed congestion.
The minutes record who raised each objection and why.

expected.rationale: A lone long life is set against population-level evidence.
It credits the signal based only on timing.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence; "cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

intro.description: A review checkpoint. A passage may contain anywhere from zero to several fallacies — read the whole thing, then mark only what is genuinely faulty.

sentences.text: Last spring the city ran a six-month pilot of the new bus route in four neighborhoods.
Ridership was logged automatically by the fare readers on every vehicle.
The transit office compared the pilot corridors against three similar routes left unchanged.
Weather and fuel prices were recorded so they could be accounted for later.
Residents could submit comments through a short weekly form.
Average commute times in the pilot corridors fell by about four minutes.
The analysts noted that a mild winter may have made part of that gain easier.
Satisfaction scores rose modestly, though the response rate was uneven.
Two of the four neighborhoods showed almost no change at all.
The report lists these limits plainly rather than glossing over them.
Given the mixed picture, the board proposes extending the pilot for another year.
A larger sample should make the seasonal effects easier to separate.
The office will publish the raw ridership data alongside its next summary.
If the extended trial shows no benefit, the route will return to its old form.
No permanent change is recommended until the follow-up is complete.

hints.text: Ask whether any claim outruns its evidence — careful reasoning is not a fallacy.
Notice the limits, comparisons, and follow-up. Look for overreach you won't find here.
This brief reasons carefully and contains no fallacy. The right move may be to mark nothing.

sentences.text: The new bus route has quickly become a point of pride for the city.
Supporters point to shorter waits and fuller buses during the morning rush.
Anyone who still opposes the route is just a selfish driver who ignores their neighbors.
The transit office has shared its ridership figures for anyone to review.
Local businesses have offered mixed but mostly positive feedback.
Critics raise fair questions about parking near the busiest stops.
Either we expand the route to every district now, or the whole system will fall apart.
City planners say a phased rollout is one option under discussion.
The budget for the next phase has not yet been finalized.
A public comment period is scheduled before any vote.
Supporters argue the early results are encouraging, if still preliminary.
We must approve the expansion immediately, before our children inherit a gridlocked, dying city.
Opponents counter that more data would strengthen the case either way.
The council has asked for a follow-up study before deciding.
Both sides agree the next report should settle several open questions.

expected.rationale: It dismisses opponents by calling them selfish rather than answering their reasons.
It offers only 'expand everywhere now' or 'collapse,' ignoring the phased option the very next sentence mentions.
It pushes immediate approval with a frightening image instead of evidence.

hints.text: Watch for a Personal Attack, a Distorted Choice, and an Emotional push — about one per paragraph.
Most sentences report facts or open questions; three do persuasive work the evidence should.
'selfish driver' = Ad Hominem; 'expand now or it all falls apart' = False Dilemma; 'gridlocked, dying city' = Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: The debate over the riverside development has grown heated this season.
One developer says a friend's project across the state succeeded, so this one surely will too.
City records show the proposal is still under environmental review.
Most of the loudest voices online already back the plan, so it must be the right call.
The planning office has posted the full application for public viewing.
Opponents have asked for a traffic study before any approval.
So the opponents apparently want the waterfront left to rot forever.
The developer notes that permits in the next county were granted quickly.
Ever since the rumor spread, nearby home prices ticked up, so the project is clearly already helping.
Appraisers caution that many factors move home prices at once.
Either the council approves the full plan tonight or the investment leaves for good.
Staff say a smaller first phase remains on the table.
A neutral consultant has been hired to review the figures.
The council will hold one more hearing next week.
Both sides expect the consultant's report to shape the final vote.

expected.rationale: A single project elsewhere is offered as proof this one will succeed.
Online popularity is treated as proof the plan is right.
A request for a traffic study is distorted into wanting the waterfront to rot.
A price uptick after a rumor is treated as proof the project caused it.
It offers only 'approve tonight' or 'lose the investment,' ignoring the smaller phase named next.

hints.text: Look for Bad Evidence, Framing by popularity, a Distorted argument, a Causation slip, and a Distorted choice.
Five sentences are faulty; the rest report records, cautions, or next steps.
friend's project = Anecdotal; 'loudest voices online' = Bandwagon; 'left to rot' = Straw Man; 'prices ticked up, so helping' = Post Hoc; 'approve tonight or it leaves' = False Dilemma.

sentences.text: You flag my typos, but your last memo was full of them, so back off.
They compared this year's numbers with the prior three years.

expected.rationale: It deflects by accusing the critic of the same fault.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque.

sentences.text: Sure, I missed the meeting — didn't you skip two last month?
The lab disclosed which trials did not show an effect.

expected.rationale: It answers a charge with a counter-charge.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque.

sentences.text: You criticize my driving, yet you got a ticket last week.
Participants were chosen at random from the registry.

expected.rationale: It dodges by pointing at the accuser's record.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque.

sentences.text: Renew my contract — I just can't handle another setback right now.
The budget includes a contingency line for overruns.

expected.rationale: It asks for renewal out of sympathy, not performance.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity.

sentences.text: Waive the late fee; it's been such a brutal month for me.
Findings were shared with an outside reviewer for comment.

expected.rationale: Hardship is offered in place of a valid reason.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity.

sentences.text: Pick my design — I worked nights on it while caring for my mom.
The contract specifies how disputes will be resolved.

expected.rationale: It appeals to sacrifice rather than the design's merits.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity.

sentences.text: Why should I cut sugar when you drink soda all day?
He says the bridge is unsafe, but he failed the engineering exam once.
Data collection paused while the instrument was recalibrated.

expected.rationale: It rejects the advice by alleging hypocrisy.
It points at a past failure rather than the current claim.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque; "an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: You say my code is messy, but have you seen your own repo?
The critic panned our film, though everyone knows he's a washed-up hack.
The plan names the metrics it will track each month.

expected.rationale: It turns the criticism back instead of addressing it.
It insults the critic instead of answering the criticism.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque; "an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: Don't tell me to save money when you just splurged on a vacation.
Why heed the doctor's diet plan? She smokes on her break.
Two analysts reached the same total independently.

expected.rationale: It deflects rather than engaging the point.
It rejects the plan by pointing at the doctor's habit.

hints.text: Look for: Personal Attack.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque; "an insult aimed at the person" → Ad Hominem.

sentences.text: Let us stay open; the staff have nowhere else to go.
We either hire ten people now or cancel the launch entirely.
The store tested the new layout in a single aisle first.

expected.rationale: It uses pity instead of a business case.
It rules out any smaller, phased hiring plan.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation, Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity; "only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: Give my essay a pass; I've already failed twice and I'm desperate.
Either the festival sells out or it's a total failure.
The report separates confirmed results from preliminary ones.

expected.rationale: It leans on desperation rather than the work itself.
It allows no outcome between sellout and failure.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation, Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity; "only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: Approve my loan — I've struggled my whole life to get here.
We either ban phones in class or surrender to constant distraction.
They published the raw data along with the summary.

expected.rationale: It substitutes a hard story for the loan criteria.
It offers only a ban or chaos, ignoring managed use.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation, Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity; "only two extreme options" → False Dilemma.

sentences.text: Profits climbed after the leadership swap, so the new boss created the gains.
You complain I'm always on my phone — so are you, constantly.
Pass on this insurance and one accident could wipe you out.
The schedule builds in time to revisit early decisions.

expected.rationale: It assumes the later success was caused by the earlier change.
It answers a complaint with the same accusation.
It pressures with worst-case fear rather than facts.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors, Personal Attack, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc; "a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: The garden bloomed after I played it music, so the music helped it grow.
He scolded me for being late, but he's never on time either.
Stall the project and competitors will bury us for good.
The estimate was checked against last year's actuals.

expected.rationale: It mistakes coincidence in time for causation.
It dismisses the scolding by citing the scolder's habit.
It uses a threat of defeat to compel agreement.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors, Personal Attack, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc; "a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: Once we moved desks, output rose, so the new layout caused the boost.
You want me to apologize? You never apologized for last year.
Doubt the forecast and you're gambling with your family's safety.
The committee invited dissenting members to submit notes.

expected.rationale: It reads cause from a change in timing.
It deflects the request with a counter-grievance.
It frames disagreement as endangering loved ones.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors, Personal Attack, Emotional Manipulation.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc; "a 'what about you' deflection" → Tu Quoque; "a threat used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: Two buses were late today, so this city's transit is completely broken.
The model's limitations are listed in an appendix.

expected.rationale: A sweeping verdict is drawn from two incidents.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization.

sentences.text: My last two dates were boring, so dating apps are a total waste.
Inspectors visited each site rather than relying on photos.

expected.rationale: It generalizes from a tiny sample.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization.

sentences.text: A few reviews mention bugs, so the whole product is unusable.
The trial recorded side effects as well as benefits.

expected.rationale: It leaps from a handful of reports to a blanket claim.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization.

sentences.text: Towns with more libraries have lower crime, so libraries cut crime.
Funding was approved in stages tied to clear milestones.

expected.rationale: It treats a correlation as proof of cause.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"linked trends read as cause and effect" → Correlation/Causation.

sentences.text: Kids who eat breakfast get better grades, so breakfast raises grades.
The team logged every change with a timestamp and reason.

expected.rationale: It ignores other factors behind the link.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"linked trends read as cause and effect" → Correlation/Causation.

sentences.text: Months with more weddings have more rainstorms, so weddings bring rain.
Results were broken out by region to spot any differences.

expected.rationale: It reads a spurious correlation as causation.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"linked trends read as cause and effect" → Correlation/Causation.

sentences.text: Three students cheated, so this entire class can't be trusted.
One graduate got hired straight from a bootcamp, so degrees are obsolete.
The author thanks reviewers who challenged the first draft.

expected.rationale: It condemns everyone based on three cases.
A single hire is used to dismiss a broad trend.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization; "a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence.

sentences.text: I had two bad meals there, so that whole cuisine is overrated.
My phone survived a drop in the lake, so this brand is basically indestructible.
A second lab repeated the measurement to confirm it.

expected.rationale: It overgeneralizes from two experiences.
One incident is generalized into a durability claim.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization; "a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence.

sentences.text: Both interns I trained quit, so interns never stick around.
A reader wrote that the tea fixed her insomnia, so it works for sleep.
The plan can be reversed if the metrics decline.

expected.rationale: It turns two cases into a universal rule.
An isolated testimonial replaces real evidence.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization; "a single story used as proof" → Anecdotal Evidence.

sentences.text: Offices with standing desks report fewer sick days, so the desks prevent illness.
She started journaling and slept better, so journaling fixed her sleep.
They surveyed both customers who stayed and those who left.

expected.rationale: It assumes the desks cause the difference.
It assumes the first habit caused the second outcome.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"linked trends read as cause and effect" → Correlation/Causation; "cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: Regions with more solar panels have higher test scores, so panels boost learning.
After the tax cut, hiring picked up, so the cut created the jobs.
The figures were rounded, with exact values in the footnotes.

expected.rationale: It links unrelated trends as cause and effect.
It infers causation from sequence alone.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"linked trends read as cause and effect" → Correlation/Causation; "cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: People who drink wine live longer, so wine extends your life.
We launched the newsletter and churn fell, so the newsletter retained users.
The committee set a date to review whether the policy worked.

expected.rationale: It mistakes a correlation for a causal lever.
It credits the newsletter based only on what followed.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"linked trends read as cause and effect" → Correlation/Causation; "cause inferred only from timing" → Post Hoc.

sentences.text: Don't fine us; we're a tiny shop just scraping by.
A couple of clients complained, so our service is clearly failing.
All the neighbors installed these panels, so they're obviously the right move.
Each recommendation is paired with its expected cost.

expected.rationale: It pleads for leniency on sympathy, not the rules.
It generalizes from a couple of complaints.
Neighborhood uptake is treated as evidence.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation, Bad Evidence, Framing & Bias.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity; "a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization; "popularity treated as proof" → Bandwagon.

sentences.text: Let me on the team; I'd be crushed to be left out again.
Two coworkers love the gym, so everyone here must be fitness-obsessed.
Every serious investor is buying in, so you'd be foolish not to.
The study notes where its data was incomplete.

expected.rationale: It appeals to feelings rather than qualifications.
It builds a sweeping claim from two people.
Crowd behavior is used to compel a decision.

hints.text: Look for: Emotional Manipulation, Bad Evidence, Framing & Bias.

"sympathy used instead of a reason" → Appeal to Pity; "a sweeping rule from too few cases" → Hasty Generalization; "popularity treated as proof" → Bandwagon.

expected.rationale: It uses exhaustion as the reason instead of a justification.
It draws a broad conclusion from two tries.
Booking volume is mistaken for quality.

intro.description: The capstone review. A passage may hold anywhere from zero to many fallacies. Read all three paragraphs, then mark only what is genuinely faulty.

sentences.text: A research team studied whether a new tutoring program improved reading scores.
They enrolled students from six schools chosen to reflect the district.
Half were assigned to the program and half to the usual curriculum at random.
Scores were measured before the program began and again at the end of the year.
The team wrote down what they expected to find before looking at the results.
On average, the tutored group gained a few points more than the control group.
The gain was smaller in the two largest schools.
The authors report the size of the effect and how uncertain it is.
They note that attendance varied and may have influenced the outcome.
All of the anonymized data was published for others to check.
The team recommends a second trial in a different district before scaling up.
They list the questions the current study could not answer.
Funding for any expansion would be released in stages tied to results.
If the follow-up shows no gain, the program will be discontinued.
The report frames its conclusion as promising but not yet settled.

hints.text: Strong, hedged evidence is not a fallacy — check whether any claim overreaches.
Each paragraph states method, limits, or next steps. There may be nothing to mark.
This study reasons carefully and contains no fallacy. Marking nothing may be correct.

passages.title: A Memo for the New Tool

sentences.text: Management circulated a memo urging every team to adopt the new workflow tool.
This bold, visionary change will leave only stubborn dinosaurs behind.
Teams that already use the tool report finishing their sprints on time.
Offices that switched tools last year also saw morale rise, so the tool clearly lifts morale.
A trial period is available for teams that want to test it first.
Some engineers worry about the time it will take to migrate.
When one manager raised that concern, a colleague shot back that he had complained about the last tool too, so why listen to him now.
The vendor offers training sessions throughout the transition.
IT has documented the migration steps for each team.
A rollback plan exists in case the switch causes problems.
At the meeting, one lead pleaded that her team had endured such a brutal quarter that the budget simply must be approved.
The finance office asked for the numbers behind each request.
A decision is expected after the next planning review.
Teams can submit written feedback until Friday.
The final rollout schedule will be shared once the feedback is reviewed.

expected.rationale: Charged words ('visionary,' 'stubborn dinosaurs') do the persuading instead of reasons.
Morale rising alongside the switch is treated as proof the tool caused it.
It deflects the concern by pointing at the manager's past complaints instead of answering it.
It argues for the budget from a hard quarter rather than the request's merits.

hints.text: Look for Loaded wording, a Causation slip, a Whataboutism deflection, and a Pity plea.
Four sentences lean on rhetoric instead of reasons; the rest describe process.
'visionary... dinosaurs' = Loaded Language; 'morale rose, so the tool lifts morale' = Correlation/Causation; 'you complained before' = Tu Quoque; 'such a brutal quarter' = Appeal to Pity.

sentences.text: The city is debating whether to fund the new downtown stadium.
Ever since the team announced the plan, downtown sales rose, so the stadium is already boosting the economy.
The mayor's office released a cost estimate for public review.
Anyone against it is just a bitter cynic who hates progress.
An independent firm was hired to check the projections.
Supporters held a rally downtown last weekend.
Either we build the stadium now or the city stays stuck in the past forever.
Planners say several sites are still being compared.
Polls in two other cities favored their stadiums, so ours is sure to be loved too.
A public hearing is scheduled for next month.
Most of the popular local accounts are sharing the plan, so it must be the right move.
Reject the funding and watch the whole downtown collapse into ruin.
Critics have asked for a detailed traffic and noise study.
The council will review the consultant's findings before voting.
Both sides expect that report to weigh heavily on the outcome.

expected.rationale: A rise in sales after the announcement is treated as proof the stadium caused it.
It labels opponents 'bitter cynics' instead of answering their objections.
It offers only 'build now' or 'stuck forever,' ignoring other options the piece itself mentions.
Two other cities are used to conclude ours will surely be loved too.
Popularity among online accounts is treated as proof the plan is right.
It pushes against rejection with a frightening image of collapse instead of evidence.

hints.text: Several moves here: a Causation slip, a Personal attack, a Distorted choice, a Hasty rule, a Popularity appeal, and a Scare.
Six sentences are faulty; the rest are neutral reports of process.

'sales rose, so boosting' = Post Hoc; 'bitter cynic' = Ad Hominem; 'build now or stuck forever' = False Dilemma; 'two cities, so ours too' = Hasty Generalization; 'popular accounts sharing' = Bandwagon; 'reject and downtown collapses' = Appeal to Fear.

sentences.text: When asked about the missed deadline, he launched into a long story about how busy traffic has been.
The proposal was compared with two alternative approaches.

expected.rationale: A deadline question is dodged by pivoting to traffic — an unrelated diversion.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"an unrelated topic used to dodge" → Red Herring.

sentences.text: Pressed on the budget overrun, she pivoted to talking about how nice the new lobby looks.
Reviewers checked the math and noted no errors.

expected.rationale: A budget question is sidestepped by switching to office decor.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"an unrelated topic used to dodge" → Red Herring.

sentences.text: Instead of answering the safety question, he reminded everyone how many awards the team has won.
The trial enrolled participants from several clinics.

expected.rationale: A safety concern is dodged by listing unrelated awards.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"an unrelated topic used to dodge" → Red Herring.

sentences.text: If we allow casual Fridays, soon people will dress for the beach on Monday and morale will collapse.
The team set thresholds for when to stop the experiment.

expected.rationale: A small dress-code change is chained to morale collapse with no supporting steps.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a chain of dire predictions" → Slippery Slope.

expected.rationale: One retake is asserted to inevitably end all grading.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a chain of dire predictions" → Slippery Slope.

hints.text: Look for: Causation Errors.
Focus on sentence 1. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"a chain of dire predictions" → Slippery Slope.

sentences.text: Our product is the best because no competitor matches its quality.
A famous actor swears by this supplement, so it must really work.
They confirmed the trend held after removing the holidays.

expected.rationale: The premise just rephrases the conclusion — being best is asserted as proof of being best.
An actor’s fame is treated as evidence in a medical question outside their expertise.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Argument Form, Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"the conclusion treated as its own premise" → Begging the Question; "an out-of-place expert vouching" → Appeal to Authority.

sentences.text: This policy must be fair, since fair policies are exactly what we adopt here.
The CEO of a different company called the plan brilliant, so we should adopt it.
The board asked for a smaller test before full approval.

expected.rationale: Fairness is assumed in the premise to prove fairness in the conclusion.
A CEO from another industry is cited as proof, with no relevant evidence.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Argument Form, Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"the conclusion treated as its own premise" → Begging the Question; "an out-of-place expert vouching" → Appeal to Authority.

sentences.text: He’s trustworthy because he wouldn’t say anything that wasn’t true.
A retired general endorsed the diet, so its science must be solid.
The notes flag which conclusions are still tentative.

expected.rationale: Truthfulness is assumed in the premise to prove trustworthiness.
Military rank is offered as a stand-in for nutritional evidence.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Argument Form, Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"the conclusion treated as its own premise" → Begging the Question; "an out-of-place expert vouching" → Appeal to Authority.

sentences.text: Three customers raved about the launch, so the rollout was clearly a hit, while the dozens who complained went unmentioned.
Skipping a stretch session is just as bad as skipping the surgery itself.
The study reports both the effect size and its uncertainty.

expected.rationale: Only favorable feedback is shown; the contrary cases are left out.
A minor habit lapse is equated with a major medical decision.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Bad Argument Form.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"only the favorable cases shown" → Cherry-Picking; "unlike things treated as the same" → False Equivalence.

sentences.text: Our best month showed strong growth, proving the strategy is working, with last quarter’s losses left off the chart.
Forgetting a coworker’s birthday is essentially the same as betraying their trust.
They checked whether the result held in a second city.

expected.rationale: Selective months are presented; losing periods are quietly omitted.
A small oversight is treated as morally equivalent to a serious betrayal.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Bad Argument Form.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"only the favorable cases shown" → Cherry-Picking; "unlike things treated as the same" → False Equivalence.

sentences.text: Two studies support our claim, so it’s settled; the eight studies that found no effect are skipped.
Being five minutes late is morally equivalent to missing the whole shift.
The plan schedules a review once more data is available.

expected.rationale: Supporting studies are highlighted while null-result studies are ignored.
A short delay is equated with full absence — very different in scale.

hints.text: Look for: Bad Evidence, Bad Argument Form.
Focus on sentence 1, 2. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"only the favorable cases shown" → Cherry-Picking; "unlike things treated as the same" → False Equivalence.

sentences.text: Asked about the typos, the editor began listing every weekend he had worked all month.
Cut the parking budget and within a year the whole campus will be impassable.
Last year’s hires from this college all succeeded, so the pipeline is perfect, with the ones who left omitted.
The team revisited the plan after the first month's data.

expected.rationale: A complaint about typos is met with a tangent about workload.
A modest cut is chained to a worst-case outcome with no mechanism.
Only successful hires are counted; those who departed are excluded.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices, Causation Errors, Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"an unrelated topic used to dodge" → Red Herring; "a chain of dire predictions" → Slippery Slope; "only the favorable cases shown" → Cherry-Picking.

sentences.text: Confronted about the bug, she shifted to praising the design team’s recent redesign.
Skip one yearly review and reviews will quietly vanish company-wide, ending accountability.
Top reviewers loved the film, so it must be a masterpiece, while the bulk of mixed reviews are quietly skipped.
Each option was scored on the same five criteria.

expected.rationale: The bug question is deflected by praising another team’s work.
Skipping one review is chained to the end of accountability everywhere.
A small set of glowing reviews is featured; the broader picture is ignored.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices, Causation Errors, Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"an unrelated topic used to dodge" → Red Herring; "a chain of dire predictions" → Slippery Slope; "only the favorable cases shown" → Cherry-Picking.

sentences.text: When the cost question came up, the speaker started reminiscing about the company’s early days.
Let kids have phones at dinner and family conversation will disappear from the world.
Five players hit personal bests after the new diet, so it’s transformative, with the rest of the squad’s results missing.
The report was updated when a calculation error was found.

expected.rationale: A cost question is brushed aside with company nostalgia.
A small habit is chained to a sweeping civilizational loss.
Standout cases are presented as the whole story; counter-cases are hidden.

hints.text: Look for: Distorted Choices, Causation Errors, Bad Evidence.
Focus on sentence 1, 2, 3. One sentence here is sound — don't mark it.
"an unrelated topic used to dodge" → Red Herring; "a chain of dire predictions" → Slippery Slope; "only the favorable cases shown" → Cherry-Picking.

intro.description: The capstone. A passage may hold anywhere from zero to many fallacies — including the subtler moves introduced in Tier 8. Read every paragraph before deciding what to mark.

sentences.text: A working group reviewed thirty years of regional rainfall and temperature records.
They compared the regional patterns against larger global averages to control for shared trends.
The data sets came from three independent agencies with different collection methods.
Any reading flagged as a sensor error was excluded with a clear note in the appendix.
The team pre-registered which patterns they expected to find before running their main analysis.
On average, the regional warming trend tracked closely with the global mean.
Rainfall variability rose more than the long-term average would predict.
The report distinguishes well-supported findings from those that remain tentative.
Two cooperative outside reviewers checked the calculations and noted no errors.
Where assumptions could swing the conclusions, the team modelled both directions.
The committee recommends another five years of monitoring before redrawing the planning maps.
Until then, only the most robust findings will guide infrastructure decisions.
A public dashboard will publish updates as new data arrive each season.
If the long-term trend reverses, the recommendations will be revised accordingly.
All raw data and code are posted for any team that wants to reproduce the analysis.

hints.text: Strong, hedged evidence is not a fallacy — check whether any claim overreaches.
Every paragraph states method, limits, or next steps. Look for overreach you do not find here.
This report reasons carefully and contains no fallacy. Marking nothing may be the right call.

sentences.text: The board met last month to discuss the proposed phone policy update.
Several teachers had written letters supporting limits on classroom phone use.
Asked about enforcement costs, the chair pivoted to a long story about last year's bake sale.
The principal shared survey data from three nearby districts.
A retired general endorsed the policy at an earlier meeting, so its educational case must be sound.
The vote drew applause from supporters in the audience.
Critics argued that better evidence would strengthen the case.
If we ban phones during instruction, families will demand bans during lunch, then on the buses, then in their own homes.
The board promised to publish the meeting transcript online.
The superintendent asked for a phased pilot before any district-wide rule.
Three classrooms participated in a short pilot last spring with mixed results.
The supporters' brief highlighted the two classrooms where attention scores rose, while leaving out the third.
A second-round pilot is scheduled for the fall semester.
The board will share the full results before making any permanent change.
Comments from families remain open until the end of the month.

expected.rationale: An enforcement-cost question is dodged by switching to an unrelated bake-sale anecdote.
A retired general's endorsement is used to vouch for an educational policy outside their expertise.
A chain of dire predictions — buses to homes — is asserted with no mechanism connecting the steps.
Only the two classrooms where scores rose are highlighted; the third is omitted.

hints.text: Look for a Diversion, a Misplaced Authority, a Sliding Chain, and Selective evidence.
Four sentences do persuasive work the evidence should — about one per paragraph plus one.
'pivoted to a bake sale' = Red Herring; 'retired general endorsed' = Appeal to Authority; 'lunch, then buses, then homes' = Slippery Slope; 'two rose, the third omitted' = Cherry-Picking.

sentences.text: The city is debating a proposal to require delivery drones to carry tracking beacons.
Supporters argue beacons would help recover lost packages and deter theft.
The drone-tracking law is the right move because tracking drones is exactly what well-run cities do.
Opponents say the cost would fall mostly on small couriers.
Failing to require beacons is essentially the same as legalizing theft from every porch.
The city's tech committee released a draft cost estimate for public review.
When asked about hardware compatibility, the sponsor changed the subject to citywide bike-lane progress.
A famous tech executive from a different industry has called the bill brilliant, which settles the question.
A working group is comparing similar laws in three other cities.
If we require beacons today, by next year we'll be tracking grocery carts and house keys, and freedom will be gone.
Three small couriers in last quarter's pilot reported smooth integration, proving the program is industry-ready.
The committee will hold a public hearing next month.
A revised draft is expected after the hearing.
Both supporters and critics have submitted written testimony for the record.
The full text and amendments will be posted online.

expected.rationale: The premise restates the conclusion — tracking is right because tracking is what good cities do.
Not requiring beacons is treated as morally equivalent to legalizing theft — very different things.
A hardware-compatibility question is dodged by pivoting to bike lanes.
An executive from a different industry is cited to settle a question outside their expertise.
A chain from drones to grocery carts and house keys is asserted without supporting steps.
Only three smooth-integration cases are highlighted; the rest of the pilot results are unmentioned.

hints.text: Six newer moves: Circular reasoning, False equivalence, Diversion, Misplaced authority, a Sliding chain, and Selective evidence.
Six sentences do persuasive work; the rest are neutral reports of process.

'right because tracking is right' = Begging the Question; 'same as legalizing theft' = False Equivalence; 'pivoted to bike lanes' = Red Herring; 'a different-industry executive settles it' = Appeal to Authority; 'grocery carts and house keys' = Slippery Slope; 'three smooth cases, rest omitted' = Cherry-Picking.
